---
title: "Notebook31"
solutions-release-date: "2026-08-31"
---

### Setup

Run all of the following before starting the notebook.

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
#| eval: false
! wget -q -nc https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/funs.py
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

ub = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/taylor-arnold/fds2/refs/heads/main/"
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
```

```{python}
#| tags: [remove]
#| include: false
ub = ""
```

```{python}
#| tags: [noclear]
it_city = pl.read_csv(ub + "data/it_cities.csv")
```

### Questions

This notebook uses a table of 388 Italian cities, `city_name`, `lon`, `lat`,
and `pop`. Every city in the table has a population over 20,000; smaller
comuni were left out. `lon` and `lat` are plain numbers for now, degrees of
longitude and latitude, not yet a geometry `geo` can work with. Most
questions below build a `geometry` column on top of them and then use it, so
unlike earlier chapters, a few answers get saved back into `it_city` instead
of only printed, the same way the chapter itself keeps reassigning `metro`
as it goes. Anything that is not a geometry-building step should still just
be printed.

1. Sort the cities by population, largest first, and print the top 10.

```{python}
(
    it_city
    .sort(c.pop, descending=True)
    .head(10)
)
```

2. Plot `lon` on the x-axis and `lat` on the y-axis with `geom_point()`, no
`geo` methods yet.

```{python}
(
    ggplot(it_city, aes(c.lon, c.lat))
    .geom_point()
)
```

**Answer**: It already looks like Italy, boot and islands and all. That is a
coincidence of the numbers, not a property of the plot. Longitude and
latitude are being read as if they were ordinary x and y coordinates, with
no geometry, no CRS, and nothing telling `plotnine` these points sit on a
curved surface. It works here mostly because Italy is a small, mid-latitude
country; the distortion this hides gets much worse for something that
stretches close to the poles.

3. Build a `geometry` column by hand with `pl.format`, the way the chapter
first showed, but write `lat` before `lon` inside the string instead of
`lon` before `lat`. Print the result.

```{python}
(
    it_city
    .with_columns(
        geometry = pl.format("POINT ({} {})", c.lat, c.lon)
    )
)
```

4. Plot that same table with `geom_map()` and `coord_fixed()`. Does it still
look like Italy?

```{python}
(
    ggplot(
        it_city
        .with_columns(
            geometry = pl.format("POINT ({} {})", c.lat, c.lon)
        )
    )
    .geom_map()
    .coord_fixed()
)
```

**Answer**: No. The outline is rotated onto its side and mirrored, nothing
like the shape from question 2. WKT points are written `POINT (lon lat)`,
longitude first, and swapping the order silently builds a valid geometry
that describes the wrong locations. Nothing in the code raised an error;
the map just quietly plotted something else. This is exactly why the
chapter moves on to `points_from_xy` instead of building WKT text by hand
for anything beyond a quick demonstration.

5. Build the geometry properly with `it_city.geo.points_from_xy("lon",
"lat")` and save the result back into `it_city`. Print it and check which
CRS got recorded automatically.

```{python}
it_city = it_city.geo.points_from_xy("lon", "lat")
it_city
```

6. In the first block, add `coord_fixed()` to the plot from question 2, so
its axes are locked to the same scale as a map. In the second block, project
`it_city` into EPSG:7791, the modern reference system for Italy, and plot
that with `geom_map()` and `coord_fixed()` too. Compare the two.

```{python}
(
    ggplot(it_city, aes(c.lon, c.lat))
    .geom_point()
    .coord_fixed()
)
```

```{python}
(
    ggplot(it_city.geo.to_crs(7791))
    .geom_map()
    .coord_fixed()
)
```

**Answer**: With the axes locked, the two look almost the same. The
chapter's own map of the continental US, Alaska, and Hawaii shifts a lot
more under projection, because it stretches from the tropics almost to the
Arctic. Italy only runs from about 37°N to 47°N, a 10-degree band where a
degree of longitude does not shrink by nearly as much as it does at the
latitudes where the US map has real problems. The projection is still the
correct thing to do, both for the reasons in the chapter's "Why projections
matter" callout and because later notebooks need it for area and distance;
it just has less visible work to do on a plot this size.

7. Make an interactive map of `it_city` with `.geo.explore()`, showing
`city_name` and `pop` in the tooltip. Zoom in on a city you already know
something about and check that the map has it in the right place.

```{python}
it_city.geo.explore(tooltip=["city_name", "pop"])
```

8. Filter to cities with a population over 500,000, the same `.filter()`
from several chapters back, then chain straight into `.geo.explore()` with
the same tooltip.

```{python}
(
    it_city
    .filter(c.pop > 500_000)
    .geo.explore(tooltip=["city_name", "pop"])
)
```

Six cities come back: Rome, Milan, Naples, Turin, Palermo, Genoa. That list
is checkable without the map. Anyone who has seen a map of Italy already
knows those are roughly its six biggest cities, in roughly that order, so a
wildly wrong pin here would be obvious before trusting anything built on top
of it later.

9. Filter to cities south of 39°N and explore the result. What part of Italy
is this, and does the cluster of points on the map match what you'd expect
geographically?

```{python}
(
    it_city
    .filter(c.lat < 39)
    .geo.explore(tooltip=["city_name", "pop"])
)
```

**Answer**: Sicily and the toe of Calabria on the mainland, 49 cities in
total. The points bunch up around Palermo, Catania, and the Sicilian coast,
with a thinner scatter across the mainland side of the strait, which is a
reasonable match for where Southern Italy's larger towns actually sit.

10. In the first block, sort by population, smallest first, and print the
bottom 5. In the second block, count how many cities in the whole table have
a population under 25,000.

```{python}
(
    it_city
    .sort(c.pop)
    .head(5)
)
```

```{python}
(
    it_city
    .filter(c.pop < 25_000)
    .select(pl.len())
)
```

**Answer**: The smallest city in the table, Guidonia, has 20,943 people, and
86 of the 388 cities, better than one in five, sit within 5,000 of that same
floor. That is not a natural break in the data; it is a cutoff someone
applied when building the table. Italy has roughly 7,900 comuni in total,
and this table only has 388 of them. A row missing from a spatial table is
just as easy to miss as a row missing from any other kind: an empty patch
on a map looks the same whether it is a real gap in the countryside or a
gap in the data.
